title: Notifications module is an empty stub — no domain model, use cases, or delivery infrastructure
body:
## Issue description

The `notifications` module (`src/modules/notifications/`) is an empty placeholder. Its public API file exports nothing:

```typescript
// src/modules/notifications/index.ts
export {};
```

No domain model, application use cases, infrastructure adapters, or presentation routes exist for the module. No other module subscribes to domain events that would trigger notifications. As a result:

- Users receive no in-app or out-of-band notifications for any event (group invitations, event invitations, event updates, membership changes, etc.).
- The notification delivery channel (push, email, in-app) is undefined and unimplemented.
- Audit-worthy operations that should trigger notifications (e.g. event invitation, event cancellation, member removal) silently complete without any user feedback beyond the immediate API response.

This gap affects the core user experience: group calendar features (accepting/declining invitations, being notified of event updates) depend on a working notification system.

## High-level requirements / Expected behavior

**Domain model:**
- Define a `Notification` entity with fields: `id`, `recipientUserId`, `type` (e.g. `event_invited`, `event_updated`, `event_cancelled`, `group_invited`, `member_removed`), `payload` (safe, minimal JSON), `createdAt`, `deliveredAt`, `readAt`.
- Define a `NotificationPort` interface for sending notifications.

**Application use cases (minimum viable set):**
- `SendEventInvitationNotification` — triggered when an `EventInvitation` is created.
- `SendEventUpdateNotification` — triggered when an event is updated or cancelled.
- `SendGroupInvitationNotification` — triggered when a user is added to a group.

**Infrastructure:**
- Implement an in-app notification store (database table) so notifications can be listed and marked as read.
- Optionally implement email delivery via Azure Communication Services or similar, using managed identity.
- All external delivery adapters must be behind injectable interfaces so they can be replaced with fakes in tests.

**API:**
- `GET /api/v1/notifications` — list unread notifications for the authenticated user.
- `PATCH /api/v1/notifications/:id/read` — mark a notification as read.

**Integration with existing modules:**
- `event-management`: publish a domain event when an invitation is created, updated, or removed; when an event is updated or cancelled.
- `group-membership`: publish a domain event when a member is added or removed.
- The `notifications` module subscribes to these domain events via the existing `event-bus.ts` infrastructure (`src/shared/event-bus.ts`).

## Additional notes

- Architecture rule: notifications must not expose event content to users who are not authorized to see the event. Notification payloads must be minimal — only the notification type and safe identifiers (e.g. `eventId`), not event titles or details that could leak information to a removed invitee.
- Push notification delivery (e.g. Web Push, Azure Notification Hubs) is out of scope for the initial implementation. Start with in-app (database-backed) notifications.
- All notification operations are auditable operations; log notification sends with the existing `audit-security` module where appropriate.
- Fake notification adapter must be provided for CI tests so no real delivery calls are made.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `Notification` domain entity and `NotificationPort` interface are defined.
- [ ] Use cases exist for event invitation, event update/cancellation, and group invitation notifications.
- [ ] Database migration adds a `notifications` table.
- [ ] `GET /api/v1/notifications` returns the authenticated user's unread notifications.
- [ ] `PATCH /api/v1/notifications/:id/read` marks a notification as read.
- [ ] Authorization: a user can only read and update their own notifications; accessing another user's notification returns `404`.
- [ ] `event-management` and `group-membership` modules publish domain events that the notifications module subscribes to.
- [ ] Notification payloads do not contain event titles, descriptions, or any data the recipient is not explicitly authorized to see.
- [ ] Fake notification adapter is used in CI; no real delivery calls are made.
- [ ] Unit tests cover notification policy (correct recipients, safe payload).
- [ ] API tests cover the list and read endpoints with the full authorization matrix.
- [ ] CI passes.
